1:
The chapter opens with all Israel streaming to Hebron, and the witnesses begin by mapping where the story has come from. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch lay out the architecture of the whole chapter: its two halves run parallel to the second book of Samuel, verses 1 through 9 answering to the narrative of 2 Samuel 5:1-10, and the register of verses 10 through 47 to the roll of 2 Samuel 23:8-39. They recall the road that led here: after Saul's death David had left Ziklag at a divine intimation, gone up to Hebron with his wives and his warriors, and been anointed by the men of Judah over their own tribe alone (2 Samuel 2:1-4), while Abner set Ishbosheth over Gilead and the north. That long, divided decline the Chronicler simply passes over.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the gathering as the divine purpose ripening at last. David is brought to the throne of all Israel after seven years reigning in Hebron over Judah only, and on three grounds: his kinship with the people, his former good services, and above all the divine designation of v.2. Henry draws his lessons. God's counsels will be fulfilled at last, whatever difficulties lie in the way; if God has said David shall rule, it is in vain to oppose it. Men who have long stood in their own light, wearying themselves with lying vanities, will at length, it is to be hoped, understand the things that belong to their peace and return to their own mercies. And he marks the covenant struck between prince and people: though David of all men might most safely have claimed absolute power, he made a covenant and took the coronation-oath, to rule by law.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the wording. "Then" is too definite a mark of time; the Hebrew is simply "and." "All Israel" proves that the reference is not to David's earlier election by Judah alone (2 Samuel 2:4); now it is the whole nation. Hebron, the burial-place of the patriarchs, was the capital of Judah, David's own tribe. And the proverb on the people's lips, "we are thy bone and thy flesh," Ellicott traces from physical to moral unity, its roots in Genesis 2:23 and Judges 9:2: David was no valiant foreigner, like certain of his own heroes soon to be named, but their own kin, whose life of struggle and success had marked him out as their divinely chosen leader. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, briefer, simply refers the reader back to the fuller history at 2 Samuel 5:1-10. The witnesses do not divide; together they frame the coronation as the appointed convergence of a nation's will and a long-declared word of God.
2:
The elders' speech reaches back before the coronation to the days when David, though not yet king, already did a king's work: "even when Saul was king, thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as the pivot on which the Chronicler turns from the anointing to the capture of Zion and the choosing of Jerusalem for the royal residence, and they trace the seam of the narrative forward: the note that David "waxed greater and greater" at v.9 opens the way to the whole history of his reign that follows.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the phrases one by one. "In time past" is literally "yesterday, or three days since," an idiom so elastic that it covers fourteen years in Genesis 31:2 and more than forty in 2 Kings 13:5. "Leddest out" points to the march to battle, "broughtest in" to the homeward return, so that the two words together confess David had long discharged the functions of a king, as the record of his early command already showed (1 Samuel 18:13). Behind the people's testimony stands the LORD's own word: "the LORD thy God said unto thee," which Ellicott refers to the anointing by Samuel at 1 Samuel 16:13.

The heart of the verse, for Ellicott, is the verb "feed." Literally it is "shepherd" or "tend," the very term used of the LORD himself in Isaiah 40:11 and Psalms 80:1. The king, then, is God's representative, his shepherd set over the flock, so that his authority is in the deepest sense divine, a point Ellicott ties to the New Testament principle of Romans 13:1. He adds a note from the monuments: the title "shepherd," applied to sovereigns, is as old as the pre-Semitic stage of Babylonian civilisation, reaching back into the second millennium before Christ, so that the image on the elders' lips was the immemorial language of kingship in the East. The two witnesses converge: David is made king not merely by the acclaim of the tribes but by the LORD's appointment, and the office he receives is a shepherd's charge, to tend the people of God as God himself tends them.
3:
The elders come in a body to Hebron, and the covenant is cut "before the LORD" before ever the oil is poured; the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the one witness the source here preserves, reads the verse as a small charter of Israel's constitution. The assembly of elders, he says, is nothing less than the Senate of Israel, met to make a contract with David concerning his prerogative and the rights of his people, thus formally determining what an earlier generation had called "the manner of the kingdom" (1 Samuel 10:25; compare 1 Samuel 8:9). Representative institutions, Ellicott observes, appear to have been the rule in the best period of Israel's national existence: the elders, the hereditary heads of the tribal subdivisions, met in council to discuss and settle matters of common concern, as the muster of 1 Chronicles 12:23 likewise shows.

The phrase "before the LORD" Ellicott takes with care. It means in the presence of the high priest, and perhaps before the ark itself, on the analogy of those passages where a matter is brought "unto God" at the sanctuary (Exodus 21:6; 1 Samuel 2:25), and where the priestly judge is even called "God" as representing the authority of the divine Judge (Exodus 22:28). The covenant, in other words, is a sacred act, sworn under the eye of heaven and not merely a political bargain struck between a captain and his tribes.

The closing clause, "according to the word of the LORD by Samuel," Ellicott identifies as a reflection added by the Chronicler himself, grounded in the facts of the old history: the rending of the kingdom from Saul and the secret anointing of the shepherd-boy at Bethlehem (1 Samuel 15:28; 1 Samuel 16:1-13). So the verse holds two things together that the modern mind is apt to sunder. On the one side, a free people freely covenants with its king and binds him by oath to rule within law; on the other, the whole transaction only ratifies a decree already spoken years before through the prophet. The elders think they are choosing; and they are; yet in choosing David they do no more than say Amen to the word of the LORD by Samuel, so that the constitutional act and the sovereign appointment prove, in the end, to be one and the same. Here the reader should note that the source's page carries also a stray fragment on Solomon's forced levy that belongs to the building of the temple, not to this coronation, and it is rightly left aside.
4:
"And David and all Israel went to Jerusalem, which is Jebus." The march on the one Canaanite stronghold never yet dislodged draws from the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the sole witness here, a close attention to the way the Chronicler has reshaped his source. Samuel is briefer, reading only, "and the king and his men went to Jerusalem, to the Jebusite, the inhabitant of the land." The Chronicler expands it in two ways. First he adds the explanatory gloss "that is Jebus," inserted, Ellicott notes, precisely because of the later mention of the Jebusite in the story that follows, so that a reader who did not know the old name would not be left behind. Then he modifies the form of the original statement, continuing "and there lived the Jebusite, the inhabitants," where the collective singular of the source becomes a plural, the whole native population brought into view.

On the name itself Ellicott gathers the philology. Jerusalem means "city of Salem"; in the Assyrian inscriptions it appears as Ursalimmu; but in Hebrew the form was so modified as to suggest "vision" or "foundation of peace," and in Greek it became Hierosolyma, "sacred Solyma," as though the very syllables of the city had been drawn toward holiness. The phrase "inhabitants of the land," he adds, is a standing name for the native Canaanites, the equivalent of the Latin indigenae or the Greek autochthones, the aboriginal dwellers whom Israel had never fully driven out.

The single witness, then, opens a wider vista than his few lines might promise. The place David and all Israel now go up against is not a foreign city but the last unconquered pocket within the promised inheritance, the old Canaanite fastness that had defied every earlier generation. Its threefold name, Ellicott shows, already carries the burden of what it is about to become: the city of Salem is to be made the city of David, and the town whose Hebrew name whispers of peace is to be the seat from which the LORD's anointed will reign. The Chronicler's careful gloss, "which is Jebus," quietly marks the hinge of the whole history, the moment the aboriginal Jebus passes into the hands of Israel's king and begins its long career as the holy city.
5:
The defenders meet David with a taunt from their walls, and the verse turns on the contrast between their scorn and the swiftness of his answer: "the inhabitants of Jebus said to David, Thou shalt not come hither. Nevertheless David took the castle of Zion." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the capture in its larger frame. This is the taking of the citadel of Zion and the choosing of Jerusalem to be the royal residence under the name of the city of David, the whole episode running parallel to 2 Samuel 5:6-10; and with it, they observe, the Chronicler opens the register of the heroes who stood by David in the founding of his kingdom, the greater part of which is found again in 2 Samuel 23:8-39, though with many divergences in the names that will occupy the witnesses through the rest of the chapter.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott concentrates on the defiant word itself. "Thou shalt not come hither" is a jeer, and he fills it out from the fuller and rougher form preserved in Samuel: "Thou shalt not come in hither; the blind and the lame will have kept thee out" (2 Samuel 5:6). The Jebusites trusted in the sheer strength of their fortress; so impregnable did they reckon it that, in their boast, even the weakest defence, a garrison of the blind and the lame, would be sufficient to repel David's assault.

The two witnesses converge on the drama the single word "nevertheless" contains. On the one side stands a city so confident in its rock that it can afford to mock; on the other, the anointed king whose coming that mockery cannot stop. The stronghold that had turned back every previous attempt, the last Canaanite islet in the land, falls at last to David, and its fall is no mere feat of arms but the founding of the city of David, the place the LORD had chosen, taken in the teeth of a contempt that only makes the conquest the more complete. What the Jebusites offered as a reason David could never enter becomes, in the event, the measure of how wholly the LORD of hosts was with him; and the citadel of their pride becomes the seat of his throne.
6:
David turns the assault into a wager of honour, and the verse fastens the reader's eye on the man who takes it up: "Whosoever smiteth the Jebusites first shall be chief and captain. So Joab the son of Zeruiah went first up, and was chief." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that here the Chronicler's account diverges more and more from its parallel. In 2 Samuel 5:8 David's word is obscure and evidently very ancient, bidding the assailant "hurl down the waterfall" (compare Psalms 42:7) the lame and the blind, "the hated of David's soul," whence the saying that the blind and lame must not enter the house. The Chronicler seems to have followed another and clearer account, one that makes Joab play at the storming of Jebus the part Othniel played at Kirjath-sepher (Judges 1:12-13), winning a great prize by a daring feat of arms. "Chief and captain" is literally "shall become a head and a captain," and Ellicott suggests it may mean head and governor of Jerusalem itself (compare v.8); "went up" means he scaled the rampart, and so "became a head." He remarks that Joab is not named at all in the parallel passage, though he already appears as David's general while Ishbosheth still reigns at Mahanaim (2 Samuel 2:13; 2 Samuel 3:23).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the offer as a lesson in what quickens resolution for great undertakings. Two things, he says, fire men to hard enterprise: opposition, for when the Jebusites set David at defiance he was the more resolved to force the place, whatever it cost; and the prospect of preferment, for when David proposed to give the general's post to whoever would lead the attack upon the castle of Zion, Joab was kindled by the proposal and went up first and was chief. Henry recalls the old soldier's proverb, "Take away honour out of the soldier's eye and you cut off the spurs from his heels."

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the exact promotion the day secured. Before this, he notes, Joab had been but one of David's chief captains; now, by being first up the wall, he is made captain-general of all the forces of Israel and Judah. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the storming of Zion is the making of Joab, and that the Chronicler, passing over Samuel's darker riddle of the blind and lame, keeps the scene fixed on the plain and stirring fact: a king's challenge, a soldier's ambition, and a fortress carried by the man who dared the climb.
7:
The captured fastness becomes at once the king's own dwelling and takes his name: "And David dwelt in the castle; therefore they called it the city of David." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over the words. "Castle" means a stronghold or fastness (compare 2 Samuel 5:7); and he marks a small nicety of the Hebrew, that whereas v.5 used the ordinary feminine form for the stronghold, here the text has the rare masculine form, a word cognate with the Arabic term for the very summit of a mountain. The place, that is, is named as a height, a citadel crowning the hill. "They called it," he adds, answers to Samuel's "one called it," both used in a general sense of what men came to say; and the "city" of the clause carries the force of the Greek acropolis, the fortified upper town.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs what the naming means. It seems, he says, that one of David's very first exploits was to make himself master of this fort, whether because he had a particular eye upon it as a place fit to be a royal city, or because he had some promise of it from God; and when he had it, he called it the city of David. To this, Henry urges, the Scripture afterward refers when it makes the LORD say, "Yet have I set my king upon my holy hill of Zion" (Psalms 2:6) so that the stronghold David wrested from the Jebusites becomes the holy hill on which God himself declares his anointed enthroned.

Between the two witnesses the verse gathers a double weight. Ellicott gives the geography and the grammar, the rock-height crowned with its citadel and newly christened for its conqueror; Henry gives the theology, the hill that men will forever after call the city of David lifted into the language of prophecy and psalm as the seat of the LORD's King. What begins as a soldier's prize, the summit taken by storm, ends as Zion, the mount of the divine decree; and the plain notice that David dwelt in the castle he had taken proves, in Henry's reading, to be the earthly ground on which the great promise of Psalms 2:6 would come to rest.
8:
With the citadel taken, David turns builder, and the labour is shared with his general: "And he built the city round about, even from Millo round about: and Joab repaired the rest of the city." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the clause literally, "and he built, or rebuilt, or fortified, the city all round, from the Millo even unto the complete round." The Millo he takes to be a tower or citadel, like the Arx Antonia of later times; and according to the Chronicler, David started from that point and brought his line of defences round again to meet it. Samuel's shorter form, "David built around, from the Millo, and inward," seems rather to mean that he carried his buildings from the fortress toward the interior of the city. Both statements, Ellicott judges, may of course be true together, the king fortifying the circuit and filling in the town within it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add a note on the last clause and its verb. The word rendered of Joab's work, they observe, is literally "to make alive," and is used here, as in Nehemiah 4:2, of the rebuilding of ruins, the raising again of what had fallen into decay. Joab, that is, restored the rest of the city, the quarters left derelict, breathing life back into the broken streets while David threw his defences round the whole.

The two witnesses, both of the same school, complete each other. Ellicott gives the shape of the work, the fortified ring drawn from the Millo around to the Millo again, and shows how the Chronicler and Samuel view the same building from opposite ends, the one from the wall inward, the other from the fortress out. Keil and Delitzsch give the sense of the verb, that this was not building on virgin ground but the reviving of a ruined town. Between them the verse pictures the founding of a capital: the king raising its ramparts and his general reclaiming its wasted interior, so that the old Jebusite stronghold is at once walled for defence and restored for habitation, and the city of David rises, whole and fortified, upon the ruins of Jebus.
9:
The section on the taking of Zion closes with a verdict that lifts the eye from walls and captains to the true source of David's rise: "So David waxed greater and greater: for the LORD of hosts was with him." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that this general remark, that David increased continually in might, is precisely the seam that opens the way to the whole history of his reign which follows, the register of heroes and the wars beyond.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott dwells on both halves of the sentence. The verse, he says, corresponds word for word with Samuel, save that it omits "God" after "LORD." The first clause is literally "and David walked on, a walking and growing great," a common Hebrew metaphor of gradual and progressive increase. But it is the title "the LORD of hosts" that draws his fullest comment. It is a contracted form of the fuller "LORD God of hosts" that stands in Samuel, and it is derived from God's supremacy over the host of heaven, that is, the stars, which the nations round about Israel worshipped as deities, so anciently conceived as an army marshalled in orderly array (Isaiah 40:26; Judges 5:20; Psalms 33:6). To call Jehovah the LORD of these hosts is therefore, in one sense, to make the concise confession that "your God is God of gods, and Lord of lords" (Deuteronomy 10:17; compare Psalms 95:3). And since the Lord of the hosts of heaven is much more the Lord of all earthly hosts, the title fits exactly a passage that ascribes David's growing greatness not to his armies but to his God.

Ellicott closes on the grand idea he finds latent in the name: that He who guides the stars in their courses guides also the destinies of individual men, elevating one and abasing another according to the eternal principles of goodness and truth (Isaiah 57:15). The two witnesses land together. David waxes greater and greater, but the sentence will not let the increase be credited to the king; the operative clause is the second, "for the LORD of hosts was with him." The Ruler of the starry armies is the strength of the rising throne, and the reader is taught, before ever the mighty men are named, to look past the heroes to the God whose presence made them, and their king, what they were. Here again the source's page has drifted into a note on the great bevelled stones of a later building; it narrates another scene entirely and is set aside.
10:
The register of the mighty men opens with a heading that gives them their whole dignity: "These also are the chief of the mighty men whom David had, who strengthened themselves with him in his kingdom." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes this catalogue an occasion for a long and generous meditation. Much of the strength and honour of great men, he observes, is borrowed from their servants and depends upon them; David is great because he has great men about him, yet the sacred writer will not let the credit rest there. "The LORD of hosts was with him, and these were the mighty men which he had"; God wrought for David, but by men and means and the use of second causes, inclining the hearts of the able to come over to his interest. The title itself, that these men "strengthened themselves with him," Henry turns into a rule of the kingdom: in strengthening David they strengthened themselves and their own cause, for his advancement was theirs, and what we do in our places for the support of the kingdom of the Son of David we shall be gainers by. The way to be truly great is to do good; and those who are faithful to the Son of David shall find their names enrolled far more to their honour than these are in the records of fame.

Henry presses further into the make-up of the band. Their exploits, he says, show the power of God, for how could one slay three hundred, and another the same, and another two lion-like men, and another an Egyptian giant, unless the LORD their God fought for them, according to the promise that one man should chase a thousand (Joshua 23:10)? He notes too that one of these worthies is an Ammonite and another a Moabite, though the law barred such from the congregation (Deuteronomy 23:3) an indication, he thinks, that the Son of David would have his worthies among the Gentiles, for with him there is neither Greek nor Jew.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the heading more exactly as "these were the heads of the warriors" who showed themselves strong in David's support, in the matter of his kingdom, together with all Israel, to make him king; a description, he notes, not given in the parallel in Samuel, where the connection is different. The clause "according to the word of the LORD concerning Israel" he ties back to v.3: David was made king both for his own sake, as the reward of his faithfulness, and for Israel's sake, that he might shepherd them with a true heart (Psalms 78:70-72). The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds the note that binds the whole together: David ascribed his success not to the hosts he had but to the LORD of hosts, not to the mighty men that were with him but to the mighty God whose presence is all in all.
11:
The roll proper begins with its foremost champion: "Jashobeam, an Hachmonite, the chief of the captains: he lifted up his spear against three hundred slain by him at one time." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch open the list and correct a misreading of its title, insisting the phrase means "the chief of the thirty," the captain set over that famous corps. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott unfolds the name and the number together. The heading in Samuel is merely "these are the names of the warriors"; the Chronicler resumes, after his parenthesis, with "these, the number of the warriors," the word "number" pointing to the corps once known simply as the Thirty. "Jashobeam" he treats as a puzzle: the Hebrew of 2 Samuel 23:8 reads very differently, and has been thought a corruption of a name meaning "Ishbosheth the Hachmonite," so that Jashobeam may itself be a disguise of Eshbaal. As for his feat, Ellicott reads it as brandishing his lance over three hundred slain in a single encounter, comparing the like exploit of Shamgar (Judges 3:31): a well-armed champion, he says, might cut down whole companies of ordinary fighting-men.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke brings the Targum's florid portrait of David seated on the throne of judgment, anointed with the holy oil, and himself overthrowing three hundred with his spear; but his weightier contribution is Kennicott's observation that the catalogue divides its thirty-seven warriors into the captain-general, a first three, a second three, and the remaining thirty, and that some thirty-four Hebrew words, happily preserved in Samuel, have here dropped out of the text.

The number itself divides the witnesses, for Samuel says eight hundred where Chronicles says three. The grammatical-historical Ellicott inclines to the larger figure as original, reasoning that since the lesser hero Abishai is credited with three hundred at v.20, the chief may well have slain more, though he grants the text of Samuel is elsewhere very faulty. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley harmonizes rather than chooses: Jashobeam slew three hundred by his own hand, and five hundred more fell to others who joined him in pursuing the victory, the two sums together making the eight hundred of 2 Samuel 23:8, the whole slaughter justly ascribed to him because it was the effect of his valour. The witnesses leave the exact tally open, but they agree in the main thing: at the head of David's mighty men stands a warrior whose single spear turned the tide of a battle, the first and greatest of the Three.
12:
Second in the first rank comes a hero named almost in passing: "And after him was Eleazar the son of Dodo, the Ahohite, who was one of the three mighties." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the witness the source preserves for this verse, gives the name its due care. For "Dodo" the Septuagint reads "Dodai," and so does the Hebrew of the parallel and the list of David's monthly captains at 1 Chronicles 27:4; the Syriac and Vulgate, taking the word as a common noun, render it "his uncle." The gentilic "the Ahohite" marks him as of the clan Ahoah, perhaps the Benjamite house of that name traced at 1 Chronicles 8:4. And the closing phrase sets his precise rank: he was "among the three heroes," one of the leading trio whose names were Jashobeam, Eleazar, and Shammah (compare 2 Samuel 23:11).

The verse, in the received text, tells only who Eleazar was, not what he did; but that reticence is itself the fruit of an accident the next verses will expose. Eleazar is introduced here as the second captain of the first ternary, and the reader is meant to pass at once to his exploit. Ellicott's naming of the trio, Jashobeam, Eleazar, Shammah, quietly prepares for the trouble ahead, for the Chronicler's text has suffered loss at just this point, and the deed that should stand to Eleazar's credit has fallen out of the record, to be recovered only by comparison with Samuel.

So the single witness lets the bare notice do a double work. On its face it simply enrols a second name in the highest class of David's men, "one of the three mighties," a title Ellicott is careful to distinguish from the lesser bands that follow. But by fixing Eleazar's place as the second of the leading three, and by tracing his father's name through its variant forms and his clan through its Benjamite line, Ellicott sets the stage for the restoration to come. The verse names a hero whose greatness the text is about to prove it has partly lost, and prepares the reader to see, in the following lines, how the flight of a whole people was turned by the hand of this son of Dodo the Ahohite into a great deliverance.
13:
Here the narrative of Eleazar's valour begins, and here too the witnesses meet a wound in the text: "He was with David at Pasdammim, and there the Philistines were gathered together to battle, where was a parcel of ground full of barley; and the people fled from before the Philistines." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies Pas-dammim, or Ephes-dammim, as lying between Shochoh and Azekah in the hill country of Judah, the very field where David had once faced Goliath. But he shows that several lines have here dropped out of the Chronicler's text, as a comparison with 2 Samuel 23:9-10 makes plain. The lost matter told how, when the men of Israel had retreated, Eleazar stood his ground and smote the Philistines until his hand was weary and clave to the sword, and the LORD wrought a great victory that day, the people returning after him only to spoil the slain. The cause of the omission, Ellicott suggests, was a copyist's eye wandering between two occurrences of the phrase "the Philistines gathered together," so that what was originally told of Eleazar the second hero, that his prowess turned the flight at Pas-dammim into a victory, has been telescoped into the exploit of a third.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, drawing on the same scholarship of Kennicott, lays the two texts side by side to the same effect, restoring from Samuel the sentence that here is missing and showing that about thirty-four Hebrew words have fallen away from this part of the Chronicler's register, words happily preserved in the older book.

The "parcel of ground full of barley," Ellicott adds, is properly the scene of the exploit of the third hero, Shammah son of Age; perhaps the Philistines were intent on carrying off the standing crop. And he flags a small textual crux even here: where Chronicles reads "barley," Samuel reads "lentils," the two Hebrew words being so alike that we cannot now tell which is right. So the verse, as the witnesses read it, is a place where the record itself has been bruised in transmission. The bare surviving clauses name a battlefield, a crop, and a rout; but behind them, recoverable only by patient comparison, stands a lost tale of one man's stand, and the confession that the God who saved Israel by a great deliverance is at work even where the text has grown dim.
14:
The exploit reaches its climax in a stand and a slaughter: "And they set themselves in the midst of that parcel, and delivered it, and slew the Philistines; and the LORD saved them by a great deliverance." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the witness here, reads the verse against the damage he has just traced in the record. The verbs, he says, ought to be singular, for in the original account this was the exploit of a single champion, Shammah, who alone planted himself in the plot of ground and defended it (compare 2 Samuel 23:12). Once the earlier omission had become fixed in the text, some later editor altered the words into the plural, supposing that they must refer to David and Eleazar of the preceding verse; and so a lone man's heroism was quietly turned into the joint act of two.

On the last clause Ellicott notes a further small difference between the books. Where Chronicles reads "the LORD saved them by a great deliverance," Samuel has "the LORD made a great deliverance"; transpose a single letter, he observes, and the Hebrew words are identical, the Septuagint and Syriac here siding with Samuel. The variation is slight, and it leaves the sense untouched: whether the LORD "saved them" or "wrought a great salvation," the victory is laid at his door and not the hero's.

The single witness, closely read, lets the verse carry more than its surface. On the one hand it is a plain record of a field held and an enemy cut down, the barley-plot that the Philistines meant to plunder defended to the death. On the other, it is a small window into how the sacred text has come down to us, its verbs shifted from singular to plural by an honest editor trying to make sense of a passage already marred, its closing word poised on the turn of a single Hebrew letter. Yet through all the accidents of copying, the theological point stands unshaken and is indeed the point the whole exploit exists to make: it was not the arm of Shammah, nor of David and Eleazar, but the LORD himself who saved them, and the great deliverance wrought in that little field belongs, first and last, to God.
15:
A fresh scene opens with a second trio and a different landscape: "Now three of the thirty captains went down to the rock to David, into the cave of Adullam; and the host of the Philistines encamped in the valley of Rephaim." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark that here begins the exploit of three others of the thirty, men whose very names have not been handed down, so that the deed is famous while the doers stay anonymous. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the wording: literally "a three out of the thirty chiefs went down," a mode of description that distinguishes this trio from the leading three already named, though the form of the verb still binds their exploit to the same war (compare 2 Samuel 23:13-17).

Ellicott gives close attention to the phrase "to the rock." The Hebrew reads "upon the rock," where Samuel has a very similar-looking phrase meaning "at harvest-time"; in Hebrew writing the two are easily confused, and our reading here looks like a correction of that in Samuel, though the Syriac, Targum, and Arabic all support the harvest-reading in their copies of the older book. The "cave of Adullam" he identifies with the refuge of David's outlaw days (1 Samuel 22:1); and the "valley of Rephaim," where the Philistine host lay encamped, he places south-west of Jerusalem, toward Bethlehem (compare Joshua 15:8). Its name may derive from the aboriginal giants, the Rephaim (Deuteronomy 3:11), and it was rich corn-land (Isaiah 13:5).

The two witnesses set the stage together. The topography is exact, the rock and the cave and the wide fertile valley now black with the enemy's tents; and the human situation is poignant, three of David's thirty coming down to their captain in his stronghold while the Philistines hold the plain of his boyhood. Keil and Delitzsch's note that their names are lost only deepens the coming pathos: the men whose devotion the next verses will immortalise are themselves left nameless, remembered not for who they were but for what their love for David drove them to dare. The scene is laid; the valley is held by the foe; and Bethlehem, just beyond, is about to become the object of a longing that will cost three men a desperate errand. Here again a stray fragment about latticed temple windows has crept into the source and is passed over as belonging to another book.
16:
The scene narrows to David in his fastness with the enemy holding his native town: "And David was then in the hold, and the Philistines' garrison was then at Bethlehem." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, gathering the whole episode of the water in one view, mark this as the setting of the famous deed of the three mightiest, and note that with the verses just after it the Chronicler passes to the second class of heroes, to which Abishai and Benaiah belong.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the two places named. "The hold" is the stronghold or rock-fortress of Adullam (compare 2 Samuel 5:17; 2 Samuel 23:14), the same refuge into which the three had gone down; and "the Philistines' garrison" is an outpost, a forward detachment thrown out from their army as it camped near Jerusalem. Bethlehem, that is, was in enemy hands, its well guarded by a Philistine post, while David lay some way off in his mountain hold.

The two witnesses, both of the same school, do little more than set the board, but the setting is everything for what follows. On the one side is David, penned in his rocky stronghold, cut off from the town where he was born. On the other is Bethlehem, so near and yet held fast by a Philistine outpost, its familiar gate and its familiar well now behind the enemy's lines. The verse names no exploit and draws no lesson; it simply places the king within sight of home and home within the grip of the foe. Yet in that bare geography the whole drama of the water is already coiled: the distance David cannot cross, the garrison that bars the way, and, unspoken as yet, the sudden homesick craving that is about to send three of his bravest through the Philistine host and back, to fetch their captain a drink from the well of his childhood. The witnesses draw no moral from the bare notice, but the very placing of David within sight of Bethlehem, and Bethlehem within the enemy's grip, is what gives the verses that follow their force; the king cut off from the town of his birth is about to be served by three men who will not let that separation stand.
17:
Out of that nearness and that barrier springs a sudden, wistful cry: "And David longed, and said, Oh that one would give me drink of the water of the well of Bethlehem, that is at the gate!" The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the well itself. No such well, he notes, is now known with certainty; the spot pointed out today as "David's well" lies about half a mile to the north-east of the town, so that the exact source of the water David craved can no longer be fixed. The phrase "that is at the gate" places it, in the old memory, hard by the entrance of the little town, the well the boy David must have known all his life.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, brief here, refers the reader to the fuller treatment of the incident at 2 Samuel 23:15-17, where the same longing and its sequel are told. The verse itself he leaves to speak its own homely pathos.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, weighing the episode among David's honours, reads the craving with a gentle candour. David longed for the water of the well of Bethlehem; it was, Henry allows, the indulgence of a foolish fancy, a homesick wish for the taste of the water he had drunk as a child, and its chief interest lies in what David will presently do with the thing when he has it. For the honour of the story, Henry insists, is not in the longing but in the mastery David is about to show over it: he that has such rule over his own spirit is better than the mighty. So the three witnesses hold the verse at the threshold of its meaning. Ellicott gives the place, now lost to certain knowledge but once so well known; Clarke points to the parallel; Henry names the longing for what it is, a natural and even indulgent craving, precisely so that the greatness of David's self-denial, when the water is brought to him at the hazard of three men's lives, may shine the brighter against it. The cry is small and human; the deed it provokes, and the refusal that crowns it, will be anything but small.
18:
The longing becomes a summons that three men answer at the risk of their lives, and David meets their gift with an act no one expects: "And the three brake through the host of the Philistines, and drew water out of the well of Bethlehem, that was by the gate, and took it, and brought it to David: but David would not drink of it, but poured it out to the LORD." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott makes plain what the three broke through, not the main Philistine army but the outpost stationed before Bethlehem; the exploit was daring but precise. And when the water is brought, David "poured it out," a phrase Ellicott takes in its technical sense, as a libation or drink-offering (compare Genesis 35:14) an act of free sacrifice, done under a sudden impulse of thankfulness and not according to any formal prescription of the law. He notes in passing, with a nod to the antiquity of such deeds of arms, that there were heroes before Agamemnon and chivalry before the Crusades.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the pouring-out as an act of devotion: the water that David thought too precious for his own drinking he poured out to the LORD for a drink-offering. If we have anything better than ordinary, Wesley draws the lesson, let God be honoured with it, who is the best and should have the best.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds in the same gesture one of the truly great things of David's whole reign, indeed reckons it, for aught he knows, as great as any exploit of the worthies here catalogued. It was, he says, an act of devotion toward God: that water which David thought too good, too precious, for his own drinking, he poured out to the LORD as an offering, honouring God with the best. The three witnesses converge on the wonder of the deed. What the mighty men win by valour, breaking through an armed post to draw a little water from a guarded well, the king will not turn to his own pleasure; he lifts it instead to God. The soldiers' courage and the sovereign's self-denial meet over a cup of water poured upon the ground, and the whole scene becomes an unbidden act of worship, the finest, Henry thinks, in the chapter.
19:
David puts his refusal into words that turn a cup of water into something sacred: "My God forbid it me, that I should do this thing: shall I drink the blood of these men that have put their lives in jeopardy? for with the jeopardy of their lives they brought it." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains the horror in the king's question. David regards the water as blood because it had been obtained at the hazard of life, and "the life is the blood" (Genesis 9:4); to drink it for his own refreshment would be, in his own eyes, to drink the very lives of the men who fetched it. Ellicott notes a small textual overloading, that the phrase "their lives" seems to have crept in twice where Samuel has it once, and that the closing summary is better read "these things did the three mighty men," the whole narrative differing only in a few words from 2 Samuel 23:13-17.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley enters into the king's feeling. It put David into the utmost confusion to think that three brave men should hazard their lives to fetch him a drink of water; in his reckoning it turned the water into blood. It is to the honour of great men, Wesley draws out, not to be prodigal of the blood of those they employ, but to hold the lives of their servants dear.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the same scene into a portrait of David's soul. Here, he says, was repentance for his own weakness, for it is really an honour to a man, when he sees he has said or done anything unadvisedly, to unsay and undo it by repentance rather than stubbornly stand to it; David repented of the rash longing that had put three lives at risk. And here was tenderness toward his servants: it threw the king into confusion that men should venture so much for so slight a thing, so that in his account the water was turned to blood, and it is the honour of great men not to be prodigal of the blood of those they employ. The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the peak of the episode, where David's refusal to drink becomes at once a confession of his own folly, a reverence for the lives of his men, and an offering lifted to God; the three mightiest have done a great thing, and the king's response has made it greater.
20:
The register turns from the first triad to the second, and to its captain: "And Abishai the brother of Joab, he was chief of the three: for lifting up his spear against three hundred, he slew them, and had a name among the three." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark the transition, noting that with this verse begins the second class of heroes, to which Abishai and Benaiah belonged, a rank below the leading three. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the man and his standing. His name appears here in the shorter Hebrew form "Abshai," beside the fuller "Abishai" of Samuel, much as "Abram" stands beside "Abiram"; and Samuel adds after "brother of Joab" the words "son of Zeruiah," pointing to the fierce house whose other deeds are recorded elsewhere (compare 1 Chronicles 18:12).

"Chief of the three," Ellicott explains, means chief of this second triad, one of whose exploits, the desperate errand to the well of Bethlehem, has just been related. The feat credited to Abishai, that he "lifted up his spear against three hundred" and slew them, is described in exactly the words used of Jashobeam at v.11 the same brandished spear, the same three hundred fallen; and by it, the text says, he "had a name among the three," that is, among the second group of which he was the head.

The two witnesses, of one school, set Abishai in his precise place in the hierarchy of David's men. He is not of the first and highest three; that rank belongs to Jashobeam, Eleazar, and Shammah. But he is the foremost of the second, a warrior whose spear could account for three hundred of the enemy at a stroke, and whose name stood at the head of his own famous trio. The careful gradation, first three, second three, and the thirty below, is not pedantry: it is the Chronicler's way of honouring each degree of valour in its own measure, so that even a hero mighty enough to slay three hundred is placed, without disparagement, one step beneath the very greatest. Abishai the brother of Joab takes his rank as chief of the second order, renowned, yet reaching not to the first.
21:
The verse weighs Abishai's honour with a nicety that the witnesses read in more than one way: "Of the three, he was more honourable than the two; for he was their captain: howbeit he attained not to the first three." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the difficult Hebrew as "honoured before the three as two," that is, doubly honoured, so that he became to his fellows a prince and leader; they note the puzzling word that Bertheau, finding it meaningless, would alter to match the reading of Samuel. And they take the occasion to survey the whole structure of the roll: after Benaiah's exploits, still to come, the men enumerated to the end of the chapter form a third class, not equal to the first triad or to the second pair of Abishai and Benaiah, and occupying a subordinate place in the royal body-guard. In Samuel, they add, thirty-two names are given, which with the three and the two of the higher classes make up the thirty-seven expressly counted at 2 Samuel 23:39; the Chronicler names no total, and swells the register with sixteen further names at its close.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the honour differently. The Hebrew here, he says, varies from Samuel, which asks "Above the three, was he not honoured?"; the Chronicler's form looks like an exegetical revision of that, and should probably be rendered "Above the three of the second rank he was honoured," making Abishai the most honoured member of the second triad, as the Vulgate too understood it. The literal "in the two," he argues, plainly points to a second group of three; and the closing clause, "he attained not to the first three," fixes the limit of his glory, for high as he stood he never reached the leading trio of v.11.

So the two grammatical-historical witnesses divide over the exact sense of Abishai's rank. Keil and Delitzsch hear in the words that he was "doubly honoured," pre-eminent over his two companions and their captain; Ellicott hears rather that he was the most honoured of the second triad, distinguished within his rank but firmly beneath the first. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley, with his usual brevity, settles only the last clause: "attained not," he says, means simply that he did not equal them. Beneath the philological difference the witnesses agree on the substance. Abishai was the chief of the second order of David's heroes, honoured above his fellows in it, yet for all his prowess he never climbed to the height of the first three, and the text is at pains to mark both his greatness and its ceiling.
22:
The register comes to its most storied figure after the triads, and heaps three exploits upon his name: "Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, the son of a valiant man of Kabzeel, who had done many acts; he slew two lionlike men of Moab: also he went down and slew a lion in a pit in a snowy day." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch introduce him at the head of the second pair of heroes and refer his deeds to the fuller commentary on the parallel at 2 Samuel 23:20-23. They fix his rank with care: like Abishai he belongs to the second class, above the thirty yet beneath the first three, a distinction the following verses will spell out.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enriches the bare catalogue with the Targum's expansive portrait of the man. Benaiah, it says, was a valiant man, fearing sin and of righteous conduct in Kabzeel; the "two lionlike men of Moab" it reads as two of the nobles of Moab, whom he cut down as though they had been two strong lions; and it makes him a great and righteous man, weaving about his memory a legend of piety, that on a certain winter's day he went down to Shiloh, broke the ice to purify himself, and went up to read the book of the law. The embroidery is plainly late and fanciful, but it witnesses to the awe in which the later tradition held this son of Jehoiada.

The two witnesses set Benaiah before us as a warrior of almost proverbial daring, the man of many acts. The plain text already gives two astonishing feats, the slaying of the two mighty men of Moab and the descent into a pit, on a day when the snow made the footing treacherous, to kill a lion trapped there; and the third, the duel with the Egyptian, waits in the next verse. Keil and Delitzsch keep him in his sober place within the ranks of David's guard; Clarke lets the Targum show how his fame grew in the retelling. Between the exact register and the later legend stands the same figure: a soldier whose courage, meeting lions and champions alike, made him one of the great names of David's kingdom, second only to the highest three.
23:
The third of Benaiah's feats is a single combat against overwhelming odds: "And he slew an Egyptian, a man of great stature, five cubits high; and in the Egyptian's hand was a spear like a weaver's beam; and he went down to him with a staff, and plucked the spear out of the Egyptian's hand, and slew him with his own spear." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, brief here, points the reader to the fuller note on the parallel at 2 Samuel 23:21, where the same duel is told.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the verse its philological weight. He renders it "he smote the Egyptian, a man of measure, five in the cubit," and observes that where Samuel says only that the man was "a sight," or "a man to look at," the Chronicler tells us why, by naming his prodigious height. The spear "like a weaver's beam" is not in Samuel, and Ellicott suspects it a recollection of the great shaft of Goliath (compare 2 Samuel 21:19), though he allows that the Septuagint's phrase, "like the beam of a ship's ladder," may be the original. "Went down," he notes, means went down to the combat, as the Latin idiom has it; and Benaiah's "staff," the weapon he happened to be carrying, is a different word from the shepherd's staff David took against Goliath (1 Samuel 17:40) so that the resemblance between the two duels, real as it is, springs from fact and not from a storyteller's imitation.

The two witnesses hold up a feat that reads almost as a second David-and-Goliath. A towering Egyptian, armed with a spear like a weaver's beam; a Hebrew coming against him with nothing but the staff in his hand; and then the sudden reversal, the champion disarmed by his own weapon and slain with his own spear. Ellicott's care to distinguish Benaiah's staff from David's, while granting the echo, guards the passage from the charge of being mere legend spun from an older tale: the likeness is the likeness of two true deeds, not of a copy to its model. Benaiah, armed with a stick, wrests the giant's spear from his grip and kills him with it, and by this third exploit takes his place among the most celebrated of all David's men.
24:
The verse gathers Benaiah's three feats into a verdict on his fame: "These things did Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, and had the name among the three mighties." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the exact force of the phrase: it means literally "to him was a name among the three heroes," that is, among the second triad, the group of which Abishai was chief. Benaiah, by the slaying of the two Moabite champions, the lion in the pit, and the giant Egyptian, had won a reputation that ranked him with that celebrated second three.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, surveying the classes of David's guard, keep Benaiah in that same careful place. He belongs, with Abishai, to the second pair of heroes, set above the thirty who follow but below the first triad; and his renown, great as it is, is the renown of that rank. The witnesses thus read the summary not as loose praise but as a precise entry in a graded roll.

The single line does the work of a seal. Where the two preceding verses narrated the deeds, this one draws the account, weighing what those deeds had earned. Benaiah had "a name," a settled fame, and it placed him among the second three; the reader is meant to feel both the height and the boundary of the honour. He is named with the mighty, listed among the heroes whose exploits were remembered and told; yet, as the next verse will make explicit, he did not reach the first three, the Jashobeam and Eleazar and Shammah whose valour stood alone at the summit. Ellicott's philological exactness and Keil and Delitzsch's structural map converge on the same modest and truthful estimate: a warrior of the highest daring, whose slaying of lions and giants had made his name a byword, takes his honoured stand just below the topmost rank, celebrated among the three of the second order. It is worth noting that the very formula used here, 'had the name among the three,' was applied a few verses earlier to Abishai as well, so that Abishai and Benaiah are bracketed together by the identical phrase, the two great names of the second rank set side by side, each renowned, and each, for all his prowess, ranked short of the first.
25:
Benaiah's rank is now stated in full, and with it comes the post that crowned his career: "Behold, he was honourable among the thirty, but attained not to the first three: and David set him over his guard." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the opening more exactly as "above the thirty, behold, he was honoured," and repeats the careful limit: Benaiah, as a member of the second triad, stood higher than the common thirty, yet reached not the first three; the third member of his own trio, he notes, is here left unnamed, just as the third of the leading triad was passed over. The closing clause he reads with precision. "Over his guard" is literally "over his obedience," an abstract standing for the concrete (as in Isaiah 11:14) and the body meant is the Cherethites and Pelethites, that small corps, probably of foreigners, which formed David's personal body-guard and stood under his direct orders (compare 2 Samuel 8:18).

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke adds the Targum's paraphrase of the appointment, that David made Benaiah "chief ruler over his disciples," and points for the rest to the notes on the parallel places where the matter is more fully weighed.

The two witnesses close the account of Benaiah on the note of trust rewarded. His valour had set him among the honoured, above the thirty though not among the first three; and to that honour David added an office of the highest confidence, command over the royal bodyguard, the household troops on whose loyalty the king's own life depended. Ellicott's care to note the omitted third member quietly underscores how the Chronicler's roll, for all its precision, has come down to us with its small gaps, the same reticence that swallowed a name in the first triad recurring in the second. But the substance is plain and honourable: the man who had faced lions and giants was set to guard the king's person, his courage in the field answered by David's confidence at home, so that the last word on Benaiah is not a feat of arms but a charge of trust, captain of the guard over the anointed of the LORD.
26:
With the triads and their captains told, the Chronicler opens the long roll of "the valiant men of the armies," and it begins with a pair: "Asahel the brother of Joab, Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, the witness the source keeps through most of this catalogue, first settles what the men are. The phrase rendered "valiant men of the armies" can bear that sense (compare 1 Chronicles 12:8) but elsewhere means simply "valiant heroes," and so here; and since Samuel names Asahel as "among the thirty" (2 Samuel 23:24), it appears that the warriors of this list are none other than the famous band of the Thirty already spoken of. Thirty, he suggests, was the original number, which became the conventional name of the corps even after its ranks had been enlarged.

Ellicott notices the pattern the list will keep. As far as v.41 the heroes are arranged in pairs, and to each name is commonly joined the town or clan from which he came; they belong mostly to Judah and Benjamin, whereas the sixteen additional names at the close, so far as they can be traced, belong to the trans-Jordanic tribes, and the northern tribes are not represented at all. Of the second name here, Elhanan the son of Dodo of Bethlehem, he raises a curious question: "Dodo" is very like "David," and he wonders aloud whether this Elhanan is yet another alias of the man credited with the slaying of Goliath (compare 1 Chronicles 20:5).

The single witness thus opens the roll with both a principle and a puzzle. The principle is that these are the Thirty, David's celebrated corps, whose names the Chronicler now enrols two by two, each tied to his native place, a muster drawn overwhelmingly from the southern tribes of Judah and Benjamin. The puzzle is the tangle of names that will run through the whole list, already sounded in the first pair, where an Asahel long dead in David's wars and an Elhanan whose father's name teasingly echoes David's own stand side by side. The catalogue is not a mere list of the forgotten; it is a roster of honour, and the witness reads it as such, the deeds mostly untold but the names preserved, each with the mark of the place that bred him.
27:
The roll continues with a second pair, and at once the variant spellings that will dog it appear: "Shammoth the Harorite, Helez the Pelonite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets each name beside its form in Samuel. "Shammoth" is there "Shammah," of which Shammoth is really the plural, and the gentilic should be "the Harodite," from a place called Harod (compare Judges 7:1) rather than the "Harorite" of our text; he notes, too, that Samuel adds after him another Harodite, Elika, a name dropped here apparently by simple accident of copying.

"Helez the Pelonite" he treats with more caution. Samuel calls him "the Paltite," perhaps more correctly, and the Syriac and Arabic point to a place-name behind the word; Beth-pelet was a town of Judah (compare Nehemiah 11:26) yet another list calls this same Helez "the Pelonite of the sons of Ephraim" (1 Chronicles 27:10). The Hebrew word "peloni," rendered "Pelonite," means literally "such a one" or "so-and-so," and Ellicott suggests it may here be a scribe's stopgap, set down where the original name had become illegible in his copy.

The single witness makes this short verse a small lesson in the fortunes of a text. Two names, and around each a cluster of uncertainties: a gentilic corrupted from Harodite to Harorite, a companion Harodite lost altogether, a Paltite turned into a Pelonite, and a "so-and-so" that may cover a name no copyist could any longer read. Yet Ellicott's patient comparison is not mere fault-finding; it is the recovery, so far as may be, of real men and real places, Harod and Beth-pelet and the hill-country of Ephraim, the homes of warriors who stood in David's Thirty. The verse shows the register at its most vulnerable to the wear of transmission, and the witness at his most useful, holding Chronicles and Samuel together so that the true shape of the names, blurred by centuries of copying, may be read again through the variants. The lost Elika and the shifting Pelonite together show how even a single short verse could gather losses and doublings enough to keep a careful reader busy; yet the patient comparison of the books keeps these two men, and their vanished companion, from passing entirely out of the record of David's guard.
28:
The next pair carries the roll into two well-known towns of the south: "Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite, Abiezer the Antothite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott identifies both men by their homes. Ira son of Ikkesh was of Tekoa, in Judah; Abiezer was of Anathoth, in Benjamin; and both, he observes, reappear in the roster of the captains who served David month by month (compare 1 Chronicles 27:9) so that these are not obscure names but men who held standing commands in the organised army of the kingdom.

The two towns fall exactly within the pattern the witness has already marked in the list. Tekoa lay in the hill-country of Judah, south of Bethlehem; Anathoth was a Benjamite town a little north of Jerusalem; and so this pair, like most of their fellows in the roll, is drawn from the two southern tribes that formed the heart of David's early support. The Chronicler enrols them, as is his manner through this stretch, two together, each tagged with the place that bred him, the deeds themselves left untold.

That silence over their exploits is itself part of the roll's character, as the register's own structure shows. These men of the Thirty are named, not narrated; unlike the triads, whose feats were recounted at length, the great body of the guard is honoured simply by the preservation of their names and homes. Yet the honour is real. Ira the Tekoite and Abiezer the Antothite were reckoned among David's mighty men, and the further notice that they led divisions of the host through the year lifts them out of the crowd: they were captains as well as champions, trusted with the command of others. The verse, brief as it is, thus does more than list two names. It roots them in the soil of Judah and Benjamin, links them to the standing organisation of David's forces, and enrols them, with the rest of the Thirty, in a record meant to keep their memory green long after the particular battles they fought had been forgotten. That two southern warriors should reappear as commanders of the yearly courses binds the roll of the mighty men to the settled administration of the realm, and reminds the reader that the champions of David's early wars became, in the ordered kingdom, the very officers who kept it.
29:
The roll comes now to a warrior whose deed, unlike most in this list, has actually been remembered: "Sibbecai the Hushathite, Ilai the Ahohite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott judges "Sibbecai" the correct form of the name (compare 1 Chronicles 27:11) and recalls that it was he who slew the giant Saph, or Sippai, in one of the Philistine wars (compare 2 Samuel 21:18). Samuel, he notes, miscalls him "Mebunnai," by an easy confusion of similar Hebrew letters. Sibbecai was a Zarhite, of the clan of Zerah, and his town Hushah lay in Judah (compare 1 Chronicles 4:4).

His companion "Ilai the Ahohite" Ellicott sets beside the form in Samuel, which reads "Zalmon," and may well be the right name, the letters having faded or been confused in one copy or the other. The gentilic "Ahohite" marks him, like Eleazar of the first triad, as of the clan Ahoah.

The single witness lets this pair stand a little apart from their neighbours in the roll, for one of the two carries a genuine exploit into the record. Where most of the Thirty are known now only by name and town, Sibbecai is remembered as a giant-killer, one of that handful of David's men who felled the great champions of Philistia, so that his name belongs with the celebrated slayers of the sons of the giant. Ellicott's care over the forms, Sibbecai against the mistaken Mebunnai, Ilai against the perhaps-truer Zalmon, again shows how the same warriors wear different names in the parallel books, the wear of transmission blurring even the heroes of the giant-wars. Yet the substance is recovered: a Zarhite of Hushah in Judah, famed for the slaying of Saph, and beside him an Ahohite of uncertain name, both enrolled among the mighty men who upheld David's throne. Sibbecai's feat sets him among that select company who put down the giants of Gath, the Saph or Sippai of the Philistine wars falling to his hand as Goliath had fallen to David's; and his clan, the Zarhites of Zerah, roots him in a known house of Judah, so that even amid the wavering of his companion's name the giant-killer himself stands firm and identifiable in the register of the Thirty.
30:
The list moves on with another southern pair: "Maharai the Netophathite, Heled the son of Baanah the Netophathite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott places both by their common home, Netophah, a Levitical canton near Bethlehem (compare 1 Chronicles 9:16) and notes that Maharai, by his family, was a Zarhite of the clan of Zerah, who is named again among the captains of David's monthly courses (compare 1 Chronicles 27:13).

The second name Ellicott judges to be more correctly "Heled" here than the "Heleb" that stands in Samuel, and he identifies him with the "Heldai" of the list of monthly captains (compare 1 Chronicles 27:15) reckoning him of the clan of Othniel. So the same man appears under three slightly different forms of his name across the records, a further instance of the small variations the witness has been tracking through the whole roll.

The single witness again roots the pair in the settled geography of the south. Netophah, near Bethlehem in Judah, sends two of its sons into David's Thirty, and one of them, Maharai, is found once more at the head of a division of the standing army, so that here too a name in the roll of champions proves to belong to a captain in the ordered host. The verse keeps the pattern of the catalogue: two warriors, one township, the exploits unrecorded, the honour resting simply in the preservation of the names. And through the play of forms, Heled, Heleb, Heldai, the witness lets us see both the fragility and the resilience of the record: fragile, because a single letter shifts from copy to copy; resilient, because by laying the lists together the true man can still be traced, a Netophathite of the clan of Othniel, enrolled among the mighty and trusted with command in the armies of the king. Netophah, a town of the Levites near Bethlehem, thus gives two of its sons to the guard, and the recurrence of Maharai among the twelve captains of the monthly courses shows once more how the roll of champions overlaps the standing army; the clan of Othniel, to which the second man belonged, carries the memory back to the old judge whose name it bears, so that even these obscure Netophathites are held within the long lineage of Israel's deliverers.
31:
The roll continues, and with this pair the witness must untangle two men from their better-known namesakes: "Ithai the son of Ribai of Gibeah, that pertained to the children of Benjamin, Benaiah the Pirathonite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that "Ithai" appears in Samuel as "Ittai," an older pronunciation, and warns that he is not to be confused with the famous Ittai the Gittite, the loyal foreigner of David's later days (compare 2 Samuel 15:19). His town, Gibeah of Benjamin, lay near Ramah, and the text is careful to assign it to that tribe.

"Benaiah the Pirathonite" Ellicott is equally at pains to distinguish from the great Benaiah son of Jehoiada, captain of the guard; this is another man entirely (compare 1 Chronicles 27:14) named from Pirathon "in the land of Ephraim" (compare Judges 12:15) which he would locate at the modern Ferata, south-west of Shechem.

The single witness makes this verse a study in the perils of a common name. Two of David's mighty men here bear names that belong also to far more celebrated figures, an Ittai and a Benaiah, and only the added detail of father and town keeps them from being swallowed up in their famous namesakes. Ellicott's care, distinguishing this Ithai of Gibeah from Ittai the Gittite, this Benaiah of Pirathon from Benaiah son of Jehoiada, preserves the identity of lesser heroes who might otherwise vanish into the record of greater ones. And the geography again is telling: a Benjamite of Gibeah and, this time, an Ephraimite of Pirathon, the one squarely within the southern heartland, the other a rare reach northward into Ephraim. The verse enrols two more of the Thirty, fixing each by his lineage and his home so that, for all the confusion of names, the men themselves are not lost among their more renowned doubles. Gibeah near Ramah and Pirathon in the hills of Ephraim mark the two poles of this pair, the one deep in Benjamin, the other a rare reach into the northern highland; and by fixing each man's town the Chronicler saves him from the confusion of his famous name, so that neither this Ittai of the guard nor this Benaiah of Pirathon is swallowed up by the greater figure he might be mistaken for.
32:
The catalogue presses on into country harder to map: "Hurai of the brooks of Gaash, Abiel the Arbathite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott prefers "Hurai" to the "Hiddai" of Samuel, comparing the name "Hur" (compare Exodus 17:10) and noting how easily the Hebrew letters for "d" and "r" are confused. "The brooks of Gaash" he explains as the gullies or wadys of Mount Gaash, a hill in the highland of Ephraim near which Joshua was buried (compare Joshua 24:30) though the exact site is no longer known.

The second name, "Abiel the Arbathite," he sets beside Samuel's "Abi-albon," and conjectures that both may go back to an original "Abi-baal," corrupted in the one text and reverently altered in the other, on the pattern by which a name compounded with Baal was later changed to avoid the idol's name. "Arbathite" he takes to mean a man of Beth-arabah (compare Joshua 15:62) a place in the desert of Judah.

The single witness again lets the verse show how much patient labour lies behind the simple reading of a name. Hurai or Hiddai, Abiel or Abi-albon or Abi-baal: each hero comes down to us in rival forms, and only by weighing the Hebrew letters and the habits of later scribes can the likely original be recovered. Yet the effort is worth making, for behind the variants are real places, the wadys under Mount Gaash in the Ephraimite hills, the Beth-arabah of the Judean waste, and real men who came from them to serve David. The verse keeps the roll's steady rhythm, a pair of warriors named and located, their deeds untold; but in this instance the very homes are half-lost, the sites unidentified, so that the witness can do no more than point toward the regions from which these two of the Thirty were drawn, and preserve, through the corruptions of the text, the memory that they were. That the wadys of Gaash and the Beth-arabah of the Judean waste can no longer be certainly located only sharpens the point: the very homes of some of David's men have slipped past recovery, and the witness can preserve no more than the direction from which they came, and the assurance, through all the corruption of letters, that such men once lived and served.
33:
The roll adds another pair, one of whom bears a name of quiet beauty: "Azmaveth the Baharumite, Eliahba the Shaalbonite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains the first gentilic as "of Bahurim," the village on the road east of Jerusalem best remembered as the home of Shimei, who cursed David there in the days of Absalom's revolt (compare 2 Samuel 16:5) a town of Benjamin, which Samuel gives in the transposed form "Barhumite." The name of his companion, "Eliahba," Ellicott renders "God hideth," a small confession of faith folded into a warrior's name; and "the Shaalbonite" he traces to Shaalbim (compare Judges 1:35) a Danite town near Aijalon.

The single witness keeps the verse within the now-familiar pattern of the catalogue, a pair of heroes fixed each by his town, the exploits passed over in silence. But two touches lift it above a bare entry. The first is the memory that clings to Bahurim, the place from which Shimei flung his stones and curses at the fleeing king; the town that would one day witness David's deepest humiliation had already given him one of his mighty men, so that the same village name carries both a hero's honour and a traitor's shame. The second is the meaning hidden in Eliahba, "God hideth," a name that speaks, however unconsciously, of the LORD who is a hiding-place for his servants, and that stands here as a reminder that these warriors were men of Israel, their very names shaped by the faith of their fathers. Azmaveth of Bahurim and Eliahba of Shaalbim take their place among the Thirty, drawn again from Benjamin and the borders of Dan, enrolled in a record that preserves not their battles but their names and homes. A stray note on the cedar wainscoting of Solomon's temple has intruded here from another passage and is left aside. Shaalbim, near Aijalon on the old Danite border, and Bahurim on the eastern road together spread the pair across Dan and Benjamin; and the meaning folded into Eliahba's name, that God is a hiding-place, gives the bare entry a quiet dignity, a soldier who carried in his very name the confession that his true refuge was not the sword but the LORD who hides his own.
34:
The roll reaches a knot of names so tangled that scarcely a word stands unquestioned: "The sons of Hashem the Gizonite, Jonathan the son of Shage the Hararite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lays the reading beside Samuel's, which has "the sons of Jashen, Jonathan," and beside the Syriac and Arabic, which read differently again. The word "sons," he judges, is an accidental repetition of the last letters of the preceding gentilic, and the true reading is probably "Jashen the Gizonite," a single warrior rather than an anonymous group of sons.

The second name he handles with equal care. "Jonathan the son of Shage the Hararite" seems to him more correct than Samuel's "Shammah the Hararite"; yet since Shammah son of Age was the third hero of the first triad (compare 2 Samuel 23:11), Ellicott wonders whether the original here read "Jonathan son of Age," the copies afterward diverging, though the Syriac and Arabic support the form "Shage."

The single witness makes this verse the very type of what the roll costs to read. Here two men, or perhaps one man and the sons of another, come down to us so muffled in variant letters that even their number is uncertain, an accidental "sons" splitting one name, a Hararite hero shadowed by the great Shammah of the first three. Ellicott does not pretend to a certainty the evidence will not bear; he weighs Chronicles against Samuel and the Syriac against both, and offers the likeliest reconstruction while owning what remains obscure. And that honesty is itself instructive. The Chronicler set down these names to honour men who had served David, and though the centuries of copying have worn some of them almost past recognition, the labour of comparison recovers enough, a Gizonite named Jashen, a Hararite named Jonathan, to keep even these battered entries from slipping wholly out of memory, still enrolled, however dimly, among the mighty men. The Syriac and Arabic, which read the names differently again, only deepen the tangle; and Ellicott's refusal to force a certainty the evidence will not yield is itself a kind of fidelity, preserving what can be recovered of these two heroes while owning honestly how much the long copying of the text has worn away.
35:
The catalogue offers another pair whose names waver between the two records: "Ahiam the son of Sacar the Hararite, Eliphal the son of Ur." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott judges "Sacar," which means "wages," to be probably the right form, against Samuel's "Sharar," and gathers the Greek witnesses, the Vatican Septuagint reading "Achar" and the Alexandrian "Sachar," with the Syriac giving "Sacham." The gentilic "Hararite" he sets beside Samuel's "Ararite," and a Syriac form again, the small consonantal shifts multiplying with each copy.

The second name he finds more seriously disturbed. "Eliphal the son of Ur" stands in Samuel as "Eliphelet the son of Ahasbai the son of the Maachathite," and Ellicott takes "Eliphelet," which was also the name of one of David's own sons, to be the correct reading, the shorter "Eliphal" being a truncation and "Ur" perhaps a fragment of the longer patronymic.

The single witness keeps the verse in the steady discipline of the roll: two heroes, each fixed by father and clan, their deeds untold, their names preserved only through the careful sifting of variants. What the verse teaches, beyond the identity of Ahiam and Eliphelet, is how the same hand that guided the register also let it pass through fallible copyists, so that a warrior's very name can shrink from Eliphelet to Eliphal and his father's from a Maachathite lineage to a bare "Ur." Ellicott's method, preferring the reading that best explains the others and noting where the Greek and Syriac diverge, is the patient art by which the men of David's guard are kept from vanishing. Ahiam son of Sacar the Hararite and Eliphelet son of Ahasbai take their place among the Thirty, their names recovered, so far as they can be, from the wear of a long transmission. That 'Eliphelet' was also the name borne by one of David's own sons lends weight to Ellicott's preference for it here, and the fuller patronymic of Samuel, tracing the man through Ahasbai to a Maachathite line, shows how much genealogy could shrink to the bare 'son of Ur' under the wear of copying; the Greek witnesses, the Vatican against the Alexandrian, and the Syriac beside them, together let the true names of Ahiam and Eliphelet be read again beneath the variants that had gathered over them.
36:
The roll brings forward a pair in which one name carries a shadow of David's later grief: "Hepher the Mecherathite, Ahijah the Pelonite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the first hero is wanting altogether in the present text of Samuel, and that "Mecherah" is unknown as a place; comparing the parallel, he suggests the original may have been "Hepher the Maachathite," a man of Maachah, whether the town of Abel-beth-maachah or the little Syrian state of that name (compare 2 Samuel 10:8).

The second name he finds even more charged. Where Chronicles reads "Ahijah the Pelonite," Samuel has "Eliam the son of Ahithophel the Gilonite" (compare 2 Samuel 15:31). "Pelonite," that word meaning "such a one," may here signal that Ahithophel's name had grown obscure in the Chronicler's copy, or, Ellicott hints, that the writer was unwilling to name the great counsellor who turned traitor; and since "Ahijah" and "Eliam" carry kindred meanings, the one "Jah is a brother," the other "God is a kinsman," they might well be forms of a single name.

The single witness lets this verse open, for a moment, onto the darker later history. Among the mighty men who upheld David's throne stood, if Ellicott's reconstruction holds, a son of Ahithophel, the very counsellor whose defection to Absalom would one day come near to undoing the king.
