Whether the Chronicler veiled the traitor's name out of reticence or lost it to a faded manuscript, the effect is the same: a household once bound to David in loyal service would furnish, a generation on, his most dangerous betrayer. The catalogue keeps its outward calm, a Maachathite and a Pelonite named in their turn; but beneath the reconstructed reading lies a quiet reminder that even David's roll of honour was not untouched by the tragedies to come, and that the line between a mighty man and a traitor's house could run through a single family. That a son of the great counsellor Ahithophel should stand here among the mighty, if the reconstruction holds, gives the verse its quiet weight; whether the Chronicler suppressed the traitor's name or lost it to a faded page, the household that once served David faithfully would furnish, in the next generation, the very counsel that came near to destroying him.
37:
The list turns again to the hill-country south of Hebron: "Hezro the Carmelite, Naarai the son of Ezbai." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes the small wavering of the first name, the Syriac reading "Hezri" and the margin of Samuel perhaps agreeing, while the text of Samuel keeps "Hezro." His gentilic "the Carmelite" marks him a man of Carmel, not the northern mountain but the town of that name south of Hebron (compare Joshua 15:55) in the hill-country of Judah.

The second name he finds more disturbed. "Naarai the son of Ezbai" appears in Samuel as "Paarai the Arbite," and Arab too was a town in the same southern district of Judah (compare Joshua 15:52) so that beneath the divergent forms may lie a single man of a single region.

The single witness holds the verse to the roll's familiar discipline: two warriors named, each tied to a place, the exploits passed over, the forms of the names weighed against the parallel. What steadies the reader here is the geography. Carmel and Arab lay close together in the hill-country south of Hebron, in the very heart of Judah, the tribe that had first made David king; and so this pair, whatever the uncertainty of their names, belongs squarely to the southern muster that fills the greater part of the list. Hezro the Carmelite and Naarai, or Paarai, take their place among the Thirty, their homes recovered even where their names blur, a further two of the men who "strengthened themselves with David" in the founding of his kingdom, enrolled by a Chronicler intent that even the least of them should not be forgotten. Here too the pattern of variants that runs through the whole roll repeats itself in miniature: Hezro against Hezri, Naarai against Paarai, the son of Ezbai against the Arbite, each name doubling as it passes from one book to another. The Chronicler, intent that even the least of David's men should not be forgotten, set them down; and though the centuries have blurred their names almost past distinguishing, the two are recovered enough by comparison to keep their place, obscure warriors of the Judean south, enrolled forever among the mighty though not a single one of their deeds survives to be told.
38:
The roll reaches into Syrian territory for one of its pair: "Joel the brother of Nathan, Mibhar the son of Haggeri." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the first name beside Samuel's "Igal the son of Nathan of Zobah," which he thinks probably the correct reading, for Zobah was a Syrian state, and a warrior drawn from so far afield would fit the widening reach of the list toward its close.

The second name he finds barely a name at all. "Mibhar," meaning "choice," is unlikely as a proper name, and Ellicott takes it for a corruption of a phrase meaning "of Zobah," while "the son of Haggeri" he reads as an easy corruption of "the Gadite"; behind the tangle, he notes, Samuel preserves the name "Bani the Gadite," which has here fallen out of the text altogether.

The single witness makes this verse another instance of the roll's later reach and its textual wear together. Where the earlier names came thick from Judah and Benjamin, here the record turns toward Zobah, the Aramean kingdom David would subdue, and toward Gad across the Jordan, as though the muster of his mighty men drew at length from the very lands his wars had brought within his sway. Yet the same widening brings deeper corruption: a hero's name reduced to the word "choice," a whole name, Bani the Gadite, lost from the line. Ellicott's reconstruction, restoring Igal of Zobah and the vanished Gadite, keeps these outlying warriors within the company; and the verse stands as a record both of how far David's fame reached, gathering champions from Syria and Gilead, and of how much the register has suffered in coming down to us, its farthest names the most battered of all. The turn toward Zobah and Gad is itself telling, for it shows the muster of David's men reaching at length beyond the old tribal heartland into the Aramean north and the pastures east of Jordan, the very lands his wars would bring under his hand. And the loss of a whole name, Bani the Gadite, fallen clean out of the line, measures how much the register has suffered just where it stretches farthest, so that the witness must labour to restore not only the forms of names but the men who have dropped from the record altogether.
39:
The roll now names outright a warrior from among Israel's old enemies: "Zelek the Ammonite, Naharai the Berothite, the armourbearer of Joab the son of Zeruiah." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott seizes on the first name to observe how many of David's warriors were aliens, ranking Zelek with Uriah the Hittite, Ittai the Gittite, and Ithmah the Moabite, who is still to be named at v.46. His companion Naharai he places at Beeroth, a town of Benjamin (compare Joshua 18:25), and notes his office as armour-bearer to Joab.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, surveying the whole catalogue, had already drawn the lesson this Ammonite invites. One of these worthies, he observes, is an Ammonite and another a Moabite, and yet the law was that an Ammonite and a Moabite should not enter into the congregation of the LORD (compare Deuteronomy 23:3). These men, Henry reasons, had so approved themselves for the interest of Israel that in their case it was thought fit to dispense with that law; and he finds in it a foretoken of grace, an indication that the Son of David would have his worthies among the Gentiles, for with him there is neither Greek nor Jew.

The two witnesses together lift this verse above a bare entry. Ellicott's cool notice, that David's guard was studded with foreigners, Hittite and Gittite, Ammonite and Moabite, becomes in Henry's hands a window onto the gospel. That a son of Ammon should stand enrolled among the mighty men of the LORD's anointed, in defiance of a law that barred his nation from the assembly, is either a scandal or a sign; and Henry reads it as a sign, the first faint light of a kingdom in which the old walls between Israel and the nations would fall. Zelek the Ammonite, whatever his story, takes his place in the roll as a pledge that David's greater Son would gather his own valiant men out of every people, and that the congregation once closed to Ammon and Moab would one day open to all who love the King. Naharai, Joab's own armour-bearer, a Benjamite of Beeroth, stands beside the Ammonite in the very same verse, native Israelite and foreign soldier serving in the one guard; and the pairing quietly reinforces the point Henry draws, that under David, and still more under David's Son, the old walls between Israel and the nations were beginning to come down.
40:
The roll adds a pair sharing a single clan-name: "Ira the Ithrite, Gareb the Ithrite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott explains the gentilic they both bear: they were of the clan of Jether, one of the families of Kirjath-jearim (compare 1 Chronicles 2:53), the town on the border of Judah and Benjamin where the ark had long rested. Two warriors of the one Ithrite house thus stand side by side in the muster.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, taking in the sweep of the whole list, fix the character of this stretch of it. Of the heroes enumerated from the second class onward, they observe, no special deeds are related; they form a third rank, subordinate to the first triad and to Abishai and Benaiah, and are honoured not by narrated exploits but simply by their enrolment in the collective body of the royal guard.

The two witnesses together tell us how to read so spare a verse. Ira and Gareb are given us with nothing but their names and their clan; of what they did in David's wars the record says not a word. Yet, as Keil and Delitzsch make plain, that silence is the settled manner of this part of the roll, and it is no dishonour. To be named here at all is to be counted among the mighty men who upheld the throne, and the Chronicler's care to record even these two Ithrites of Kirjath-jearim, otherwise unknown, shows the value he set on the whole company. Ellicott's tracing of their clan to the families of Kirjath-jearim gives them a home and a kindred; Keil and Delitzsch's note on the third class gives them their rank. Between them the verse stands as a quiet monument: two men of one small clan, their battles long forgotten, their names kept forever in the register of those who served the anointed king. To be enrolled here at all, without a deed to their name or a further word of their story, is honour enough; the Chronicler's care to preserve even two obscure Ithrites of one small clan shows that in his eyes the roll of David's men was itself a monument, and that no name once written in it, however slight, was to be let fall.
41:
With this name the older list closes and the Chronicler's additions begin: "Uriah the Hittite, Zabad the son of Ahlai." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks the weight of the first name and the silence around it. Uriah the Hittite is here enrolled among the mighty men with no hint of his story, that dark history of David's sin against him being omitted by the Chronicler altogether (its record left to 2 Samuel 11). The list of heroes in Samuel, Ellicott observes, closes with this very name, adding the summation "all, thirty and seven"; the further names that follow in Chronicles may therefore indicate a later revision of the catalogue, and are given nowhere else.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley makes the same point plainly. Uriah is the last of the catalogue in 2 Samuel 23:39; but here some others are added to the number, men, he judges, who though not of the original thirty were yet of great valour and renown among David's commanders, and so were fittingly enrolled.

The two witnesses agree in reading this verse as the seam of the register. Down to Uriah the two accounts run together, and Samuel there sets its total, thirty-seven; from Zabad onward the Chronicler carries the roll further with sixteen names his source did not contain. And the appearance of Uriah just at the join gives the moment a solemn undertone. The loyal Hittite whose murder was David's deepest crime is honoured, without comment, among the king's bravest; the man David wronged stands enrolled in David's own book of the mighty. The Chronicler, intent on the honour of the guard and not on the sins of the king, simply lets the name stand where the old list had placed it, and passes on to the added worthies, so that Uriah's fidelity is remembered even where his wrong is left unspoken. The number Samuel appends, thirty and seven, closes the older roll on a note of completeness, and the sixteen names the Chronicler adds beyond it read as a later gathering of worthies his source had passed over; that Uriah should stand exactly at the join, honoured among the faithful in the very book of the king who wronged him, gives the seam of the register a gravity the Chronicler never pauses to explain.
42:
The added names open with a captain of the eastern tribes: "Adina the son of Shiza the Reubenite, a captain of the Reubenites, and thirty with him." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the description as "a head, and thirty with him," or beside him, and considers what that following of thirty may mean. If, as seems likely, the "thirty" were the officers of David's guard of six hundred warriors (compare 2 Samuel 15:18), each captain leading about twenty men, then Adina's corps is mentioned perhaps because it was larger than the usual command.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the number the same way: the "thirty" were thirty captains who served under Adina as their colonel, so that he stood at the head not merely of a file of soldiers but of a band of officers, a commander of commanders among the men of Reuben.

The two witnesses agree that the verse marks a shift in the roll. With Adina the register crosses the Jordan into the eastern tribes, and it also changes its manner, for where the earlier names came in bare pairs, here a man is given with a following, a captain and his thirty. Whether those thirty were rank-and-file or officers, the notice lifts Adina above the simple champions of the western list: he was a leader of Reuben, entrusted with a body of men larger, it may be, than most. Ellicott's calculation from the guard of six hundred and Wesley's plainer "thirty captains under him" come to much the same thing, a warrior of standing in the trans-Jordanic muster. So the first of the Chronicler's added names sets the tone for the sixteen: men of Reuben and Gad and the eastern country, drawn into David's honour-roll alongside the heroes of Judah and Benjamin. If the thirty were indeed the officers of that guard of six hundred which had followed David from his outlaw days, then Adina commanded not a squad but a body of captains, a man of real weight among the men of Reuben; and with him the character of the roll shifts, for where the western names came in bare pairs, the eastern muster is headed by a captain given with his following, the added sixteen opening on a note of command.
43:
The eastern names continue with a pair: "Hanan the son of Maachah, and Joshaphat the Mithnite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott can do little with the second gentilic but record its uncertainty: the Septuagint gives "the Mathanite," or "the Bethanite," and the Syriac reads differently again, so that the true form of Joshaphat's designation is now past recovery. The name of Hanan's father, Maachah, points, as often in this closing stretch of the roll, toward the region east of Jordan or the Aramean border.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, viewing the whole tail of the list, remind us what to expect of it. These later names, they note, belong to the sixteen with which the Chronicler enlarges the register beyond the thirty-seven of Samuel, men whose deeds are nowhere related and who form, with the rest of this third class, the subordinate body of the royal guard.

The two witnesses together set this thin verse in its place. Hanan and Joshaphat are given us with almost nothing to hold, a father's name and a gentilic already corrupt in the oldest copies; of their exploits there is not a syllable. Yet the Chronicler thought them worth enrolling, and Keil and Delitzsch's observation explains why the record can seem so bare: this is the added portion of the list, the eastern and outlying names, kept for the honour of the men rather than for any tale of their valour. Ellicott's candour about the lost form of "the Mithnite" is the proper humility before so worn a text; he will not invent a certainty the manuscripts deny. And so the verse stands, two more names rescued from oblivion, obscure and battered, but still enrolled among those who strengthened David's kingdom, a testimony that the Chronicler's honour-roll reached even to the least-remembered of the king's mighty men. The two names come to us with almost nothing to hold, a father's name and a gentilic already past certain reading; yet the Chronicler thought them worth the enrolling, and Keil and Delitzsch's placing of them among the added eastern worthies explains the bareness, for this is the outlying portion of the list, kept for the honour of the men and not for any tale of their valour that has survived.
44:
The roll gathers a small cluster from beyond the Jordan: "Uzzia the Ashterathite, Shama and Jehiel the sons of Hotham the Aroerite." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the first by his town: the Ashterathite is a man of Ashtaroth (compare 1 Chronicles 6:56), a city in the trans-Jordanic territory of Manasseh; and the Aroerite is of Aroer, whether the Aroer of Reuben or that of Gad (compare Joshua 13:16).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms and refines the identifications. Ashtaroth he places as a town in Bashan (compare Joshua 13:25); the name "Jehiel" he notes is really "Jeuel" in the Hebrew, with "Jeiel" in the margin; and "Hotham," he adds, is a misprint of the Authorised Version for the true form of the father's name. Aroer, he agrees with Keil and Delitzsch, existed both in Reuben and in Gad, so that the gentilic points somewhere into the eastern pastures.

The two witnesses, of one school, together map this verse firmly onto the trans-Jordanic country. Ashtaroth in Bashan, Aroer in Reuben or Gad, these are the towns of the eastern tribes, the very region from which, as the earlier witness observed, the Chronicler's added names are drawn. Uzzia and the two sons of Hotham thus swell the muster from beyond the river, a father sending both his sons into David's honour-roll. Keil and Delitzsch give the geography, Ellicott the fine corrections of name and reading; and between them the verse enrols three more warriors of the eastern marches, otherwise unknown, their homes recovered from the parallel place-names, their memory kept in the register of the mighty men though their deeds have left no other trace. Ashtaroth, an old city of Bashan in the far north-east, and Aroer, whether on the Arnon in Reuben or in the land of Gad, together carry the roll firmly into the trans-Jordanic pastures of the eastern tribes; and a single father, Hotham, sending both his sons Shama and Jehiel into the guard, shows how a whole household could be drawn into David's service. The two witnesses labour over the small things, a misprinted name, a marginal reading, that these men of the eastern march might be set down rightly, and so kept from the oblivion that swallows the unnamed.
45:
The eastern muster continues: "Jediael the son of Shimri, and Joha his brother, the Tizite." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the reading forward as part of the closing group of added names, the sixteen with which the Chronicler extends his roll beyond the older list, men of the eastern and outlying regions whose deeds are nowhere told.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds a possible identification of the first brother: this Jediael, he suggests, may be the Manassite who came over to David at Ziklag in the days of his outlawry (compare 1 Chronicles 12:20), one of those who forsook Saul's cause to cast in their lot with the fugitive. If so, his enrolment here among the mighty men is the later honour of an early loyalty.

The two witnesses, both of the grammatical-historical school, let this brief verse open a small vista back into David's hard beginnings. Keil and Delitzsch keep it in its place among the added trans-Jordanic names, the third class of the guard; Ellicott, by linking Jediael to the Manassite of Ziklag, hints that some of these obscure later names belonged to men whose devotion reached back to the wilderness years, when following David was a danger and not a distinction. Two brothers, Jediael and Joha, of the otherwise unknown Tizite family, take their place in the register; and if the elder is indeed the deserter from Saul who found David at Ziklag, then the verse quietly rewards a faithfulness shown long before the crown was won, enrolling among the mighty of the established kingdom a man who had chosen David when he had nothing to give. If Ellicott is right that this Jediael is the Manassite who came over to David at Ziklag, then the verse rewards a devotion shown in the hardest days, when to follow the fugitive was to court Saul's wrath and win nothing but exile; and his enrolment here, in the roll of the established kingdom, is the late honour of an early faith. The two brothers of the otherwise unknown Tizite house thus take their place among the added eastern names, undistinguished by any recorded deed, yet held in the register because the Chronicler would let no servant of the king, however hidden his loyalty, drop unremembered from the account.
46:
The roll names another cluster, and among them a second man from Moab: "Eliel the Mahavite, and Jeribai, and Joshaviah, the sons of Elnaam, and Ithmah the Moabite." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott suggests that this Eliel may be the Gadite who joined David (compare 1 Chronicles 12:11), and that "the Mahavite" is probably a corruption of "the Mahanaimite," from Mahanaim in Gad, keeping the verse within the eastern country. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reach the same judgment on the gentilic, reading behind the strange form the name of Mahanaim, in the land east of Jordan (compare Joshua 13:26), and they carry forward the last name of the group, Ithmah the Moabite, without emendation.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, weighing the catalogue as a whole, had marked just such a name as this. As one of David's worthies was an Ammonite, so another, he notes, was a Moabite, though the law shut both nations out of the congregation of the LORD; their admission he reads as a token that the Son of David would have his worthies among the Gentiles, for with him there is neither Greek nor Jew.

The three witnesses together give the verse both its geography and its grace. Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch fix its men in the eastern land, the sons of Elnaam and their fellows drawn, like most of the added names, from Gilead and the region of Mahanaim; and Henry lifts the last name into the light of the gospel. Ithmah the Moabite, enrolled among the mighty men of the LORD's anointed, is a living exception to the old exclusion of Moab, and so a quiet promise. The kingdom of David, even in its roll of soldiers, already reaches past the borders of Israel; and the Moabite who stands here among the valiant is a pledge of the wider kingdom in which the door once barred to his people would be opened, and men of every nation gathered to the greater King. Mahanaim, in the land of Gad east of Jordan, gives the sons of Elnaam their likely home, so that this cluster too belongs to the eastern muster with which the roll draws toward its end; and the Moabite among them stands as the second of those aliens whose presence Henry had read as the first dawn of a wider grace.
47:
The long register closes on three names half-lost in the corruption of the text: "Eliel, and Obed, and Jasiel the Mesobaite." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dwell on the last, puzzling gentilic. "The Mesobaite," they observe, is not in form a proper clan-name at all; one older scholar took it for a contraction of a place-name near Hebron, while Bertheau judged that the article had crept into the text by mistake, and that the remaining consonants recall the "Mezobaite" of the parallel (compare 2 Samuel 23:36). Either way the word is a riddle, the very last designation in the roll defeating certain reading.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the word is corrupt, and offers the likeliest guess: perhaps it should be read "of Zobah," carrying the list, as so many of its closing names, into the Syrian borderland; the name "Eliel" too, he notes, the Septuagint gives as "Daliel," a further small uncertainty at the very end.

The two witnesses bring the great catalogue to a close on a note that is somehow fitting. Its last three warriors, Eliel, Obed, and Jasiel, are given us with a gentilic no scholar can confidently render, the text worn thinnest just where it ends. Yet the Chronicler set them down, and the witnesses labour over them, precisely because the roll is a record of honour, and no name in it is too obscure to be worth recovering. From Jashobeam who slew three hundred to Jasiel of the unreadable town, the register has moved from the blaze of the first triad's exploits to the near-silence of these final names; but all alike are enrolled among the men who "strengthened themselves with David" to make him king. The muster that opened with the anointing of all Israel ends with three half-known warriors of the eastern march, and in that long descent from the famous to the forgotten lies the whole point of the list: that the King's mighty men, greatest and least, are every one of them remembered. Here the source's page dissolves into the machinery of the website that carried it, and that residue is rightly left behind.
